metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile AC3's commit-count formula with the 'Commit-count math' section: replace `ceil(N/256)` with `sum_over_passes(ceil(pass_events/256))` (or provide a concrete per-test N/pass breakdown so both formulae yield the same number in the specific synthetic scenario used).
findings.finding: AC3 states 'commit count == ceil(N/256)' but the plan's own 'Commit-count math' section shows the correct total is sum_over_passes(ceil(pass_events/256)) — for N=3000 with two passes this is 24, not ceil(3000/256)=12. The proving command will assert the wrong expected value, causing a test that either always fails or is written to the wrong formula.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria – AC3; 'Commit-count math (per-pass flush)' section
findings.suggested_fix: Replace AC3's formula with: 'commit delta == sum over independent passes of ceil(pass_event_count / 256); for a synthetic import of N tickets with 1 CREATE + 1 COMMENT each, delta == ceil(N/256) + ceil(N/256) == 2·ceil(N/256)'. Then the proving command becomes: `python -c "import math; assert delta == 2*math.ceil(N/256)"`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile AC3's commit-count formula: replace `ceil(N/256)` with `sum_over_passes(ceil(pass_events/256))` (or equivalently, specify the concrete expected delta for the synthetic test case, e.g. 'delta == 24 for 3000 CREATEs + 3000 COMMENTs') to match the 'Commit-count math' section.
findings.finding: AC3's commit-count formula (`ceil(N/256)`) directly contradicts the 'Commit-count math' section's formula (`sum_over_passes(ceil(pass_events/256))`). For the example given (N=3000 CREATEs + 3000 COMMENTs), AC3 implies 12 new commits but the math section computes 24. An implementer writing the benchmark assertion would not know which formula to verify against.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC3 vs. 'Commit-count math (per-pass flush)' section
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC3's formula to: 'produces a real tickets branch with a commit-count DELTA == `sum_over_passes(ceil(pass_events/256))` (e.g. 24 for 3000 CREATE + 3000 COMMENT events across two passes), as defined in the Commit-count math section'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix AC3's commit-count formula to match the per-pass-flush math: replace `ceil(N/256)` with `sum_over_passes(ceil(pass_events/256))` and update the example (e.g. 'for a synthetic file of N creates + N comments, delta == 2·ceil(N/256)').
findings.finding: AC3's commit-count formula states the delta equals `ceil(N/256)`, but the plan's own commit-count math section demonstrates the actual delta is `sum_over_passes(ceil(pass_events/256))` — e.g. 24, not 12, for N=3000 with passes 1 and 2d. The AC's observable proof is arithmetically inconsistent with the plan's stated batching model, meaning a correct implementation would fail AC3 as written.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria – AC3; Commit-count math (per-pass flush) section
findings.suggested_fix: AC3 should read: 'produces a real tickets branch with commit count delta == sum_over_passes(ceil(pass_events/256)) (e.g. for a synthetic file with N=256 CREATEs and N=256 COMMENTs across passes 1 and 2d, delta == ceil(256/256) + ceil(256/256) = 2 commits instead of 512 per-event commits)', and the proving command should assert this exact per-pass sum.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for the byte-identity sub-claim in AC3, e.g. run the same NDJSON through both the batched importer and a per-event reference run, then assert `git -C <tracker_a> show HEAD:tickets/ | sort` equals `git -C <tracker_b> show HEAD:tickets/ | sort` (or equivalent `diff -r` on the two tracker working trees after import).
findings.finding: AC3 asserts 'replays to state byte-identical to the per-event path' but provides no concrete proving command or artifact comparison to verify this claim — an executor has no specified mechanism to confirm byte-identity.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit proving command for AC4: capture `git -C <tracker> rev-list --count HEAD` before and after a second import of the same NDJSON, then assert delta == 0 (no new commits) and assert ticket-count is unchanged.
findings.finding: AC4 ('idempotent re-import skips already-imported events — no duplicate events, no extra commits') has no concrete proving command: the only global command given is `pytest tests/ -k import -q`, which may or may not exercise re-import idempotency, and no git command is specified to verify 'no extra commits'.
findings.suggested_fix: Example command sequence: `BEFORE=$(git -C <tracker> rev-list --count HEAD) && import_tickets <same_file> && AFTER=$(git -C <tracker> rev-list --count HEAD) && [ "$AFTER" -eq "$BEFORE" ] || (echo 'FAIL: extra commits on re-import' && exit 1)`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate ALL intermediate-layer functions that must propagate `buffer=` from the importer's public API call-sites (`rebar.create_ticket`, `rebar.edit_ticket`, `rebar.link`, `rebar.comment`, `rebar.set_file_impact`, `rebar.set_verify_commands`) down to `_seam.append_event`, and add each intermediate file (`_lib_writes.py`, `composer.py`, `leaf.py`, `graph/_links.py`) to the plan's edit-target list.
findings.finding: The `buffer=` kwarg propagation chain from the importer's high-level API calls to `_seam.append_event` traverses ~10 intermediate functions across ~6 files that the plan never names as edit targets. The importer calls `rebar.create_ticket`, `rebar.edit_ticket`, `rebar.link`, `rebar.comment`, `rebar.set_file_impact`, `rebar.set_verify_commands` (all in `_lib_writes.py` / `rebar.__init__.py`), which in turn call `composer.create_core`, `composer.edit_core`, `leaf.comment`, `leaf.set_file_impact`, `leaf.set_verify_commands`, and `graph._links.add_dependency` — none of which currently accept a `buffer` parameter. The plan claims the opt-in mechanism works 'at the `_seam.append_event` layer', but the importer does not call `_seam.append_event` directly; the buffer kwarg must be threaded through every intermediate layer for the mechanism to function.
findings.location: ## Deferred-commit opt-in (mechanism) section; Scope section ('Introduce the compose/commit split at the `_seam.append_event` layer')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a Scope sub-section listing the full propagation chain: `_lib_writes.py` (`create_ticket`, `edit_ticket`, `comment`, `set_file_impact`, `set_verify_commands`), `_commands/composer.py` (`create_core`, `edit_core`), `_commands/leaf.py` (`comment`, `set_file_impact`, `set_verify_commands`), and `graph/_links.py` (`add_dependency`, `_write_link_event`) all need `buffer: list[tuple[str,dict]] | None = None` added and forwarded. Alternatively, consider a different mechanism (e.g., a context-var or thread-local buffer collector) that avoids threading the kwarg through every intermediate layer.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that `buffer` propagation is scoped to only the leaf/graph helpers the importer actually calls (create_core, edit_core, _write_link_event, comment, set_file_impact, set_verify_commands), not all helpers that internally call `append_event`.
findings.finding: The plan propagates the `buffer` kwarg through ALL leaf/graph write helpers that call `append_event`, but the importer only ever calls a subset (create_ticket, edit_ticket, link, comment, set_file_impact, set_verify_commands). Adding `buffer` to helpers the importer never uses — tag, untag, archive, signing, workflow/recorder, sidecar, session_log, unlink — gives those helpers an unused parameter, violating YAGNI.
findings.location: Deferred-commit opt-in (mechanism) section: 'a new optional keyword arg on the `_seam.append_event` funnel (and the leaf/graph write helpers that front it)'
findings.suggested_fix: Limit `buffer` kwarg propagation to the six helpers the importer invokes. All other helpers (tag, untag, archive, signing, workflow/recorder, sidecar, session_log, unlink) keep their existing signatures unchanged — they are already covered by the 'every non-importer caller is unchanged' guarantee from the `buffer=None` default at the `_seam.append_event` level.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC3's synthetic NDJSON to include at least one intra-batch adversarial link pattern (e.g. a pair that would form a cycle, and a duplicate link), and document in the plan whether intra-batch guard stale-state is a known limitation (with mitigations such as flushing between conflicting events) or is proven impossible by import data structure.
findings.finding: The compose/commit split may silently break link-cycle and idempotency guards when two or more link events in the same Pass 2b batch interact: guard N+1 reads committed state that does not yet include buffered (uncommitted) event N, so a cycle or duplicate that only manifests within the batch goes undetected. The plan asserts guards 'run before the event is composed' and read current committed state, but does not validate this is safe when intra-batch interactions exist.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) document that the import source guarantees no intra-batch link conflicts (and enforce with an assertion), or (b) flush the Pass 2b buffer and recommit before each link event whose guard depends on state written by an earlier buffered event in the same pass, or (c) add an explicit adversarial case to AC3's synthetic fixture and confirm the guard still fires correctly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how intra-buffer guard staleness is handled for Pass 2b link events (e.g., maintain an in-memory shadow of pending link state alongside the buffer, or reduce batch size to 1 for pass 2b, or document that the NDJSON source guarantees no intra-batch duplicate/cycle pairs and add an assertion).
findings.finding: State-reading guards (link cycle detection, `_is_active_link`, hierarchy promotion) read COMMITTED state at the time they run, but within a 256-event batch they run before any of the batch is committed. Events 2–256 in the same pass 2b buffer see stale state that doesn't reflect events 1–(N-1) already appended to the buffer, creating a TOCTOU window: duplicate links or missed cycle violations within one batch are silently admitted.
findings.location: Scope section ('Guards are preserved; only the final git commit is deferred') and Pass 2b description ('guarded by cycle detection, hierarchy promotion, and idempotency (_is_active_link) — those guards READ current state')
findings.suggested_fix: For Pass 2b, maintain a lightweight in-memory set of (src, dst) pairs already appended to the current buffer and check it alongside the committed-state guard before appending; flush and reset the set with each batch_stage_and_commit chunk.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a failure-handling specification for mid-flush errors: define whether the importer catches the exception and surfaces a clear error (with the count of committed vs. pending events), whether a partial flush is safe to retry via source_id idempotency, and confirm source_id records are committed atomically with their corresponding event chunks.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify a recovery path if `batch_stage_and_commit` fails mid-flush (e.g., after committing chunk 1 of 12 but before chunk 2). The importer would have partially committed state, and a subsequent re-import relies on source_id idempotency to skip already-committed events — but the plan never confirms that the source_id mechanism survives a partial batch commit correctly, nor that the importer surfaces the error clearly rather than silently leaving partial state.
findings.location: Scope section ('flush each buffer through `batch_stage_and_commit` in chunks') and AC: 'Idempotent re-import (source_id re-scan) still skips already-imported events'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or broaden AC5) to run the full test suite (not just `-k import`) or explicitly cite existing tests that cover the `_seam.append_event` default (`buffer=None`) path, ensuring the shared funnel's unchanged behavior is regression-tested.
findings.finding: The test criterion (`pytest tests/ -k import -q`) filters to import-related tests only, but the compose/commit split is introduced at `_seam.append_event` itself — a shared funnel used by non-importer callers. Regressions on the default `buffer=None` path (existing callers) would not be caught by import-filtered tests alone, and the plan does not cite existing test coverage for the default path.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria – AC5; Deferred-commit opt-in (mechanism) section
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC5 to: `make lint && make typecheck && python -m pytest tests/ -q` (drop the `-k import` filter), or add a separate AC: 'Existing non-importer callers of `_seam.append_event` (covered by `pytest tests/ -k "not import"`) continue to pass with no changes to their commit behavior.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `batch_stage_and_commit` and `batch_write_and_push` exist in `src/rebar/_store/event_append.py` (B1 must be merged) before any B2 implementation begins, and confirm the exact module path and signature match what the plan calls.
findings.finding: The plan's hard prerequisite (`_store.batch_stage_and_commit`) does not exist in the codebase: neither `batch_stage_and_commit` nor `batch_write_and_push` are present in `src/rebar/_store/event_append.py` or anywhere else. B1 ticket 890d is `in_progress` with its own plan-review signature showing `dep absent tests/unit/test_store_batch_write.py`, confirming the B1 implementation is not yet merged. The plan treats `_store.batch_stage_and_commit` as an available API to call in AC2 and Scope, but this is an unverifiable 'already exists' assertion.
findings.location: Scope section ('Introduce the compose/commit split … flush each buffer through `batch_stage_and_commit`'); AC2 ('flush via `batch_stage_and_commit`'); 'What' section ('Adopt the B1 batch primitive (`_store.batch_stage_and_commit`…)')
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit gate: 'Implementation blocked on B1 merge. Once merged, verify the function signatures at `rebar._store.event_append.batch_stage_and_commit(tracker, items)` and `batch_write_and_push` before writing any B2 call sites.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly address the `relates_to` reciprocal LINK write in `add_dependency` (graph/_links.py lines 264–267): clarify how both the forward and reciprocal `_write_link_event` calls are captured in the buffer during Pass 2b.
findings.finding: The plan does not mention that `add_dependency` in `graph/_links.py` writes TWO LINK events for `relates_to` relations (lines 262–267: a forward `_write_link_event` plus a reciprocal one). With the buffer mechanism, both LINK events must be captured in the buffer, but the plan's description of Pass 2b only discusses the single-event path, leaving the reciprocal write unaddressed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the buffered Pass 2b handles `relates_to` reciprocal-link deduplication: since `_is_active_link` reads disk and cannot see in-buffer events, add an in-buffer seen-set that tracks both the forward and would-be-reciprocal (target_id, source_id, 'relates_to') links so `add_dependency`'s reciprocal guard is not the only line of defence.
findings.finding: In buffered mode, `add_dependency`'s reciprocal-link guard (`_is_active_link(target_id, source_id, ...)` at _links.py:264) reads disk AFTER writing the forward link to the in-memory buffer — but the forward link is not yet committed. For a `relates_to` import where BOTH directions (A→B and B→A) appear in the source data, neither direction's forward-link event is on disk when the other's reciprocal guard runs, so both calls will conclude 'no active reciprocal' and each will emit a spurious second reciprocal LINK event, producing 4 LINK events instead of 2.
findings.location: src/rebar/graph/_links.py lines 256–267; plan section 'Scope' / Pass 2b description
findings.suggested_fix: In the importer's pass 2b buffer loop, after calling `link(...)`, also add `(tlocal, local, 'relates_to')` to `seen_links` whenever the relation is `relates_to`. This prevents the second import call from entering `add_dependency` at all, side-stepping the disk-read guard entirely and matching the behaviour of the per-event path where the first commit is visible before the second guard runs.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the mechanism spec how `edit_core`'s two-event path (EDIT + TAG_DELTA) propagates the `buffer` kwarg — both `append_event` calls inside `edit_core` must receive the same caller-supplied buffer, requiring the `buffer` arg to be threaded into `edit_core` itself (not just into `_seam.append_event`), or alternatively document that tag operations in `edit_ticket` are excluded from buffering and will still commit per-event.
findings.finding: The plan specifies the `buffer` kwarg propagates through `_seam.append_event` and 'the leaf/graph write helpers that front it', but `edit_core` in composer.py can call `append_event` TWICE in a single invocation (once for the EDIT event at line 410, once for a TAG_DELTA event at line 424). The plan does not specify how the buffer kwarg threads through `edit_core`'s multi-event path — particularly whether both `append_event` calls receive the same buffer, or whether tag-delta buffering is in scope for pass 2a (which uses `edit_ticket(parent=…)`).
findings.location: src/rebar/_commands/composer.py lines 409–430; plan section 'Deferred-commit opt-in (mechanism)' and Pass 2a description

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a planned ADR or CLAUDE.md section documenting the compose/commit split pattern: opt-in semantics (`buffer=None` default), the invariant that state-reading guards run before any append, buffer-ownership rules, and which callers may and may not use the deferred path.
findings.finding: The compose/commit split at `_seam.append_event` is a novel architectural pattern (a new deferred-commit contract added to the project's core write seam), but the plan includes no planned ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc entry to capture the decision, its rationale, and its constraints (opt-in semantics, guard ordering, buffer ownership).
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-paragraph 'Architecture Decision' section to the ticket (or reference a CLAUDE.md entry to be created) covering: (1) why opt-in rather than opt-out, (2) the guard-before-append invariant and how it is enforced, (3) which passes may use deferred commit and which must remain per-event.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either derive the 256 default from a measurement or documented heuristic (e.g., 'chosen to keep peak memory under X MB at Y bytes/event'), or expose it as a module-level constant (e.g., `_BATCH_SIZE = 256`) so it can be overridden at a single location without a config key.
findings.finding: The 256-event chunk size is hardcoded with an explicit 'no new config key' policy, but this threshold directly controls the tradeoff between git commit overhead and memory usage and may need operational tuning (e.g., for very large imports, slow git backends, or memory-constrained environments) without any code-change escape hatch.
findings.location: ## Scope — 'default 256, hard-coded — no new config key'; Commit-count math section
findings.suggested_fix: Define `_IMPORT_BATCH_SIZE: int = 256` as a module-level constant in `import_ndjson.py` (not a config key), document its derivation in a comment, and reference it in the plan so future tuning is a one-line change.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that composing the event dict (including `hlc.next_tick`, `env_id`, `author`) at buffer-fill time and committing it later does not violate HLC monotonicity or author-provenance invariants — confirm against `_seam.append_event` lines 182-192.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `_seam.append_event` already supports (or will trivially support) a `buffer: list[tuple[str, dict]] | None = None` opt-in parameter, treating this as a straightforward addition — but the current `append_event` signature (`ticket_id, event_type, data, tracker, *, repo_root, author_fallback`) has no such parameter and its implementation directly calls `_store_append.write_and_push` with no branching. Adding a `buffer` mode requires the function to compose an event dict WITHOUT calling write_and_push and return it — a structural change the plan does not verify against the current code structure.
findings.location: 'Deferred-commit opt-in (mechanism)' section; AC1 ('a command can return its composed `(ticket_id, event_dict)` without committing')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief 'Alternatives considered' note in the plan ruling out `git fast-import` (or equivalent bulk-object-write approach) on the grounds that it bypasses the Python write lock (I5), state-reading guards, and the HLC/UUID envelope composition that guards must execute before any event is staged.
findings.finding: The plan does not acknowledge `git fast-import` — the canonical git mechanism for bulk-loading many file objects into a repository under a single commit — before committing to the compose/buffer/flush architecture. While the codebase's locked-write invariant (I5) and state-reading guards (cycle detection, idempotency) plausibly exclude `git fast-import`, the plan never makes that ruling-out explicit, leaving the prior-art search visibly incomplete.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'Scope' or a new 'Alternatives considered' subsection: '`git fast-import` was ruled out: it would bypass the Python write lock (I5), preventing state-reading guards (cycle detection, `_is_active_link`, HLC monotonicity) from running before each event is staged. The compose/buffer/flush approach preserves the full locked-write invariant while reducing commit overhead.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
